model_output:
The question compares the Indus Valley Civilization (also known as the Harappan Civilization, flourishing around 2600–1900 BCE) with Ancient Rome (whose major advancements in infrastructure began around the 6th century BCE onward). The key is identifying which achievement of the Indus people predated a similar Roman one by approximately 2000 years. Based on historical analysis, the Indus Valley is renowned for its urban planning, including advanced sanitation and drainage systems in cities like Mohenjo-Daro and Harappa, which included covered drains and indoor plumbing as early as 2500 BCE[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation)[[2]](https://historyfacts.com/world-history/article/indus-valley-civilization-was-more-advanced-than-rome). In contrast, Rome's Cloaca Maxima (a major sewer system) dates to around 600 BCE, and their aqueducts to 312 BCE—putting the Indus ahead by roughly 1900–2000 years in sophisticated sewage systems[[3]](https://www.facebook.com/61559028458252/posts/centuries-before-romes-aqueducts-or-europes-sewage-systems-the-indus-valley-civi/122190417440300948)[[4]](https://www.quora.com/What-were-the-major-cultural-and-technological-achievements-of-the-ancient-Indus-Valley-civilization)[[5]](https://byjus.com/free-ias-prep/important-facts-about-indus-valley-civilization-for-upsc-exams).

Let's briefly evaluate the options to confirm:

- (A) Sewer systems: Matches the timeline and is a well-documented Indus innovation that outpaced Rome[[2]](https://historyfacts.com/world-history/article/indus-valley-civilization-was-more-advanced-than-rome).
- (B) Standardized bricks: The Indus did use uniform baked bricks for construction around 2500 BCE[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation), but Romans also standardized bricks by the 1st century BCE—not a 2000-year gap.
- (C) Paved road systems: Romans excelled here (e.g., Appian Way in 312 BCE), while Indus streets were planned but not a vast intercity network like Rome's.
- (D) Concrete in monumental building: This was a Roman invention (opus caementicium) around 200 BCE; the Indus didn't use concrete.
- (E) First professional standing army: Rome professionalized its army under Marius in 107 BCE, but earlier examples exist (e.g., Assyrians); the Indus civilization appears largely peaceful with no evidence of a standing army.

The strongest match for a 2000-year lead is in sanitation/sewage systems.

<final_answer>
(A)
</final_answer>
